title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: For questions 9 - 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The development of vaccination by Edward Jenner in 1796 marked a turning point in the history of medicine, (0) which laid the foundation for the systematic control and eventual eradication of several infectious diseases. Its importance to public health cannot be overstated. (9) Although Jenner's method of protecting against smallpox using material from cowpox was initially met with both interest and scepticism, it soon demonstrated its effectiveness against one of the most feared diseases of the era. (10) Such was the success of the resulting vaccination campaigns that smallpox, once responsible for hundreds of thousands of deaths annually, was declared eradicated by the World Health Organisation in 1980. Immunisation programmes require a substantial proportion of the population to participate in order to achieve herd immunity, and (11) Provided that vaccine coverage is maintained above critical threshold levels, even individuals who cannot receive vaccines for medical reasons gain indirect protection. Not (12) only did Jenner's discovery save countless lives in the centuries that followed, but it also established a scientific framework for understanding how the immune system can be primed to resist future infections. (13) Regardless of the precise mechanism, which Jenner himself could not have fully explained in the absence of germ theory, the practical results of his intervention were clear. Not (14) until Koch and Pasteur developed the germ theory of disease in the late nineteenth century did scientists obtain a theoretical explanation for why vaccination worked. (15) Nevertheless, vaccine hesitancy remains a significant challenge for public health authorities in many countries, driven by a complex mix of misinformation, distrust and cultural resistance. The scientific consensus on the safety and efficacy of vaccines, however, is overwhelming. (16) Whether the challenge of overcoming hesitancy is primarily one of communication, trust or policy, the stakes for global health are too high to permit complacency.

example.text: The development of vaccination by Edward Jenner in 1796 marked a turning point in the history of medicine, (0) which laid the foundation for the systematic control and eventual eradication of several infectious diseases.

questions.explanation: Concessive conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession between two clauses.
Emphatic determiner in the inverted structure 'Such was/is the [noun] that...' to express a high degree.
Conditional conjunction meaning 'on the condition that', used to introduce a condition and its result.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'Not only... but also', which requires subject-auxiliary inversion after 'Not only'.
Part of the fixed prepositional phrase 'Regardless of', meaning 'without being affected by'.
Part of the 'Not until' negative fronting structure, which requires subject-auxiliary inversion.
Formal adverbial connector meaning 'in spite of what has just been said', introducing a contrast after positive information.
Conjunction introducing two alternatives or possibilities in a summarising conclusion.
